headline: World's Oldest Tortoise Caught in Viral Crypto Death Scam

title.text: Jonathan, the world’s oldest tortoise, has lived through the Victorian period and two world wars. He was caught in a viral cryptodeath scam where a fake X account posed as his vet sparked global false reports of his death while soliciting crypto donations
title.visual_subject: A photograph of Jonathan, the Seychelles giant tortoise, on the grounds of the governor's mansion on Saint Helena

intro.script_text: Jonathan, the world’s oldest known land animal, has lived a remarkable life spanning over 140 years. But his longevity was put to the test when a fake X account posed as his vet sparked global false reports of his death and solicited cryptocurrency donations.
intro.visual_subject: A photograph of Jonathan's shell glimmering from the morning rain

key_points.script_text: The fake X account, posing as Jonathan's vet, claimed that he had passed away and was asking for cryptocurrency donations.
key_points.visual_subject: A screenshot of the fake X post claiming Jonathan's death

key_points.script_text: However, checks by the Guardian revealed that the account was based in Brazil and not affiliated with Jonathan's real vet.
key_points.visual_subject: A photograph of Nigel Phillips, the governor of Saint Helena, searching for Jonathan on the grounds

key_points.script_text: Jonathan is alive and well, and his caretakers have been working to ensure his continued health and happiness.
key_points.visual_subject: A photograph of Jonathan being fed fruit, veg, and salad by one of his keepers

summary.script_text: Jonathan, the world's oldest tortoise, has been the victim of a viral cryptodeath scam. However, thanks to the efforts of his caretakers and checks by the Guardian, it has been revealed that he is alive and well.
summary.visual_subject: A photograph of Jonathan lying on the ground, slightly shrouded in shrubbery
